exemplar.title: Effect of Background Music on Ability to Encode and Recall Words

detailedExemplarData.title: Effect of Background Music on Ability to Encode and Recall Words

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, wellâstated aim linked to realâworld study habits and the Working Memory Model
Aim relevance explicitly explained with practical importance
Hypotheses (both null and research) correctly state and operationalize IV and DV
weaknesses: Central executive description contains factual inaccuracies (does not âconvert STM to LTMâ)
Connection between the Working Memory Model and the experiment lacks depth
Relevance paragraph could include more specific educational applications or broader implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets all topâband descriptors: the aim is clear, relevant, and explained; the theory/model is described and linked to the investigation; and the hypotheses correctly identify and operationalize variables. Minor conceptual errors do not undermine the overall completeness and clarity, warranting full marks (6/6).

strengths: Research design is not only named but its choice (counterbalanced repeated measures) is well explained
Participant selection criteria are ethically justified and transparently described
Materials and controlled variables are listed with clear rationale for control
weaknesses: Justification for convenience sampling is inaccurate and lacks critical evaluation
Volume control lacks objective units (e.g., decibels)
Procedure omits detail on randomization or counterbalancing of word lists
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design and materials strands are fully explained, demonstrating understanding of methodological choices, and sampling is at least correctly described. This fulfills all criteria for the top band (3â4) in Exploration, justifying a full score of 4/4. The minor sampling and reporting issues do not preclude the highest mark.

strengths: Appropriate choice of nonâparametric Wilcoxon signedârank test given data characteristics
Descriptive statistics (means and SDs) are clearly tabulated
Graph directly addresses the hypothesis with correct format
weaknesses: Graph misses axis titles and overprints numerical labels
Pâvalue is reported imprecisely (stated as pâ¤0.04) and sample size inconsistency is present
No effect size is calculated or reported
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates correct application of both descriptive and inferential statistics and presents the findings graphically in support of the hypothesis. Minor presentation and reporting errors limit the work to the upper range of the 3â4 band, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed directly in relation to the Working Memory Model and prior research
Strengths and limitations are relevant, explained, and linked to validity concerns
Proposed modifications are explicitly tied to identified limitations and well justified
weaknesses: Discussion lacks exploration of alternative cognitive explanations or broader implications
Some feasibility considerations for proposed modifications are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation fulfills all topâband descriptors: theoretical integration in findings discussion, explanations of strengths and limitations, and fully justified modifications linked to limitations. Despite some lack of broader perspective, it earns 5/6 for solid, relevant critique and improvement proposals.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph of mean recall scores with error bars is provided, axes are labelled, and the graph directly contrasts the two conditions, addressing the hypothesis.  Nonetheless, numerical values overlap the bars and there is no title on the axes, reflecting minor presentational faults.  Hence the work sits in the 3-4 band and earns 3 marks.

The student states the statistical outcome, rejects the null hypothesis, and links the result to the predicted effect of articulatory suppression.  Interpretation is present but brief, with little quantitative discussion of effect size or confidence.  Therefore, the strand meets the 3-4 band with a mid-level mark of 3.

Both research and null hypotheses are written, the IV (presence of Blinding Lights background music) and DV (number of words correctly recalled) are identified and operationalised with precise details (specific song, 10 four-letter words, 3-second exposure).  This meets the top-band descriptor.  Minor wording issues (e.g., repetition of âarticulatory controlâ) do not hamper clarity, so a score of 5 is justified.

The sampling method (convenience from mentor groups) and participant characteristics are listed, but the justification offered (âreduces researcher biasâ and is ârepresentativeâ) is inaccurate for convenience sampling and not critically examined.  Because the choice of technique is not convincingly explained, the work remains at the descriptive level, meriting the top of the 1-2 band.

The Working Memory Model is described with its major components, but there are factual inaccuracies (e.g., stating the central executive "converts STM to LTM"), and no mention of capacity limits or empirical support.  The link from the model to the present investigation is asserted (dual use of phonological loop and articulatory control) yet not elaborated in detail.  Because description is present but the connection is only briefly sketched, the work fits the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the noted conceptual errors.

Findings are discussed in relation to both the WMM and previous research (Perham & Sykora), showing theoretical integration.  The discussion is, however, surface-level and omits alternative explanations or broader implications, situating it in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

The student clearly states an aim â to test whether background pop music impairs free recall of 10 four-letter words â and links this to real-world study habits.  The paragraph that follows explicitly argues the practical importance for students and for understanding working memory.  Although the relevance is not developed in depth (e.g., no specific educational applications or broader social value), it is more than simply identified, satisfying the rubric requirement for an explanation.  Hence a low-to-mid score in the 5-6 band is appropriate.

Several strengths (e.g., pilot study, repeated measures) and limitations (fatigue, environment, sampling bias) are identified and explained with reference to their potential impact on validity.  The explanations are relevant but not deeply analytical, fulfilling the descriptor for 3-4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

Controlled variables (word exposure time, slide order, start point/volume of music) and materials (consent forms, standardised script, slideshow) are listed with concise rationales for control.  While some explanations are brief, they show an understanding of why each factor could influence recall accuracy.  This places the strand in the explanatory 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 reflects the limited depth.

Two concrete modifications (matched-pairs design to reduce fatigue; using the same room to control extraneous stimuli) are directly linked to previously stated limitations and justified.  Because the suggestions are clearly aligned and rationalised, the strand fits within the 3-4 band.  The level of justification is acceptable but not exhaustive, warranting 3 marks.

The student not only names a repeated-measures design but explains why it was chosen (control participant variability) and how counterbalancing mitigates order effects.  This moves beyond description into explanation, warranting the upper band.  The explanation is clear and accurate; therefore the strand earns the higher mark (4) within the 3-4 band.

Mean and standard deviation are calculated and justified; an appropriate non-parametric Wilcoxon signed-rank test is selected for repeated measures interval data.  However, the write-up contains minor inconsistencies (stated pâ¤0.04 without exact calculation, small sample size reported as 17 in the output) raising doubts about full accuracy.  Therefore the strand fits the 3-4 band, with a score of 3 acknowledging correct choice but some error risk.

annotations.comment: The description of the central executive inaccurately states it âconverts STM to LTM.â The CE coordinates subsystems rather than performing long-term encoding. Revise to reflect its monitoring role and cite empirical support for subsystem functions.
The aim is clearly linked to the WMM by explaining how background music might overload the phonological loop and impair articulatory control in free recall.
To strengthen the introduction, elaborate the link between the WMM and your experiment by explaining how dualâtask interference occurs in articulatory control versus phonological store.
The relevance paragraph could benefit from specific educational applications or recommendations for study strategies rather than a general statement about studying with music.
The hypotheses are well framed, with both research and null statements and correctly operationalized IV and DV details (song, number of words, exposure time).
The rationale for counterbalancing in the repeated measures design is clearly explained, showing how it mitigates order effects while maintaining participant consistency.

The claim that convenience sampling reduces researcher bias is inaccurate; this method often introduces selection bias. Discuss limitations of representativeness and potential alternative sampling methods.
Participant inclusion criteria are well justified (age, hearing/vision, language fluency) and linked to ethical consent, enhancing methodological transparency.
You control presentation timing and script standardization well; consider also controlling time of day or participant fatigue by scheduling sessions evenly.
Materials are thoughtfully chosen: white background prevents color confounds, standardized script reduces variability, and music start point aligns with lyrical content.
The volume control cited as â5â lacks objective units. Specify decibel levels or device settings to ensure reproducibility.
The choice of mean and standard deviation is well justified given interval data without outliers, demonstrating sound understanding of descriptive statistics.
Procedure lacks detail on how wordâlist order was randomized or counterbalanced to avoid listâspecific difficulty effects. Clarify assignment of word sets.
Consider matching word lists for lexical frequency or concreteness to ensure equal difficulty across conditions.
The table of descriptive statistics is clearly formatted, with means and SDs for each condition allowing straightforward comparison.
The bar graph omits axis titles and places overlapping numerical labels on bars, reducing clarity. Add clear labels and avoid overprinting.
Include a descriptive graph title and label both axes (e.g., âMean words recalledâ) to ensure your graph directly communicates its variables.
Choosing the Wilcoxon signed-rank test for non-parametric repeated measures is appropriate given your data characteristics.
Reporting pâ¤0.04 with critical W-value conflates significance thresholds. Provide the exact p-value from software output for precision.
Consider calculating and reporting an effect size (e.g., r for Wilcoxon) to convey the practical impact of your findings.
The discussion effectively links your results to the WMM and prior Perham & Sykora findings, demonstrating integration with background theory.
The discussion omits alternative cognitive explanations (e.g., individual differences in music processing). Including these would deepen your evaluation.
Consider discussing how individual preferences or familiarity with the song might moderate interference effects.
Ensure consistent formatting throughout references by adhering strictly to a recognized style (e.g., APAâ7), including italics and punctuation.
Linking matched-pairs design and same-room testing to previously stated limitations shows justified, relevant modifications.
The proposal to control room environment twice repeats without clear feasibility analysis. Flesh out logistical steps and resource considerations.
Implement a standardized room-booking protocol so all participants experience identical extraneous stimuli, improving internal validity.
The conclusion concisely restates your significant result (p<0.04) and reiterates its support for the WMM.
The conclusion does not acknowledge study limitations or propose future research directions. Including these elements would strengthen closure.
Several references lack complete publication details (e.g., access date for Spotify source). Verify each entry matches your chosen style guide.
